aliases: Braised pork belly in soy sauce

english: Braised pork belly in soy sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pork belly food product entry; no specific braised/soy-sauce preparation node exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers cooked pork belly generically; no soy-braised specific facet available. Closest parent match accepted.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for braised pork belly as a prepared dish. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of lor bak in Singapore is approximately 150 g, representing two to three slices of braised pork belly served over rice or as a side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Lor Bak (Braised Pork Belly) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo database entry for Lor Bak (braised pork belly) used directly. All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this entry with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at ~12.5 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central pork belly cooked composition (NDB 10124) as HPB does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lor bak. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on analogous braised pork preparations with negligible carbohydrate content (1.6 g/100 g). Foods with carbohydrate below 5 g/100 g yield very low glycemic loads regardless of GI; GL per 150 g serving = (25 × 1.6 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.6. Analogous reference: cooked pork with soy sauce, GI ~20–30 range per University of Sydney GI Database category for low-carbohydrate meat dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (553 mg/100 g) from soy sauce braising liquid, combined with high saturated fat (~12.5 g/100 g) contributing to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit lor bak to a maximum portion of 60–80 g, avoid consuming the braising sauce, request reduced-sodium soy sauce preparation, and trim visible fat to reduce both sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (1.6 g/100 g) and zero sugar are favourable, but extremely high total fat (34.2 g/100 g) and saturated fat (~12.5 g/100 g) impair insulin sensitivity and increase caloric density
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes may consume lor bak in small portions (60–80 g) as carbohydrate load is minimal, but should trim visible fat, avoid the braising sauce to limit sodium, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to improve the overall meal glycaemic and metabolic profile per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~12.5 g/100 g) raises LDL cholesterol; high sodium (553 mg/100 g) elevates blood pressure; cholesterol content (~72 mg/100 g) adds additional cardiovascular burden
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat lor bak as an occasional food only, limiting portions to 50–60 g, trimming all visible fat, avoiding the braising sauce, and balancing the meal with unsaturated-fat sources and high-fibre vegetables in line with AHA dietary recommendations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork belly is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); saturated fat at ~12.5 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid lor bak during flares; if consumed, limit to 50 g infrequently, trim all visible fat, and pair with anti-inflammatory foods such as ginger and garlic already present in the recipe.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lor bak (卤肉 / 滷肉) is a classic Singaporean Chinese braised pork belly dish with deep Hokkien and Teochew culinary roots. Pork belly — the layered cut combining skin, fat, and lean meat from the underside of the pig — is slow-braised in a fragrant soy sauce mixture containing dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, garlic, sugar, and often five-spice powder or star anise, producing a rich, deeply savoury, slightly sweet, and intensely umami dish. The long braising process renders the fat layers soft and gelatinous while the lean meat becomes tender and deeply coloured. Lor bak is commonly served sliced over steamed white rice, accompanied by braised hard-boiled eggs and tofu, with the braising sauce (lor) ladled over the rice. Nutritionally, lor bak is energy-dense at 383 kcal/100 g, driven by its very high fat content of 34.2 g/100 g (of which approximately 12.5 g is saturated fat). Protein is moderate at 17.3 g/100 g. Carbohydrate is negligible at 1.6 g/100 g with zero sugar, making the glycaemic load extremely low. Sodium is elevated at 553 mg/100 g due to the soy sauce braising liquid. Clinically, lor bak is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium and saturated fat content, and should be consumed only in small portions by those managing type 2 diabetes or rheumatoid arthritis. It is a culturally significant hawker and home-cooked dish widely available across Singapore's hawker centres, coffee shops, and zi char stalls.

Is lor bak high in sodium?

How much fat is in lor bak?
What is the difference between lor bak and lu rou fan?
meal_context: accompanied by braised eggs and tofu

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database with confidence 0.95 for an exact named dish match. Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions adapted from USDA FoodData Central pork belly cooked entry (NDB 10124) as HPB does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions; this adaptation is documented. GI is an analogous estimate for a very low-carbohydrate meat dish, which is standard practice for such foods.
notes: sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database entry for lor bak; soy sauce contributes negligible residual sugar at the dish level as reported. Saturated fat (12.5 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (14.8 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (3.6 g/100 g), trans_fat_g (0.1 g/100 g), cholesterol_mg (72 mg/100 g), potassium_mg (270 mg/100 g), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.4 mg/100 g), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.2 mg/100 g), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (3.8 mg/100 g), vitamin_b6_mg (0.3 mg/100 g), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.6 mcg/100 g), phosphorus_mg (160 mg/100 g), magnesium_mg (18 mg/100 g), and zinc_mg (2.1 mg/100 g) adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork belly cooked (NDB 10124) as HPB Foodo does not publish disaggregated fatty acid or full micronutrient profiles for this dish. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, and selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in either HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this preparation and reliable values cannot be confirmed without laboratory analysis. The dietary tag 'plant_based' present in the input record has been removed as it is factually incorrect for a pork belly dish. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for lor bak exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026.
